conditions.title: Treating students fairly

criteria.text: The provider must, if registered, treat each student fairly in relation to any activities that are connected with the provision of higher education and/or ancillary services.
criteria.threshold: Fair treatment of all students in all activities connected to higher education and ancillary services provision
risk_indicators: Student complaints or legal actions alleging unfair treatment or discrimination
Contractual terms or policies that disproportionately disadvantage certain student groups
Marketing materials or communications that contain misleading or deceptive information
Failure to address consumer protection violations or regulatory enforcement actions

criteria.text: The provider will be deemed not to satisfy C5.3 if, in the reasonable opinion of the OfS, its actions or omissions (including proposed or likely actions or omissions) fall within one or more of the following categories: a. they fall within one or more of the descriptions provided for in the OfS prohibited behaviours list; or b. they give rise to a likelihood of detriment or actual detriment to the student (unless the OfS considers that the detriment would be reasonable in all the relevant circumstances).
criteria.threshold: Actions must not be on prohibited behaviours list or cause unreasonable detriment to students
risk_indicators: Practices matching descriptions in OfS prohibited behaviours list
Evidence of student detriment from provider policies or practices
Terms and conditions that create unreasonable burden or disadvantage for students
Complaints demonstrating pattern of unfair treatment across multiple students

criteria.text: The provider will be deemed not to satisfy C5.3 if it has been subject to adverse findings under one or more of the following forms of wrongdoing in a context that directly or indirectly relates to the provision of education and/or ancillary services, unless it can demonstrate that it has addressed any issues related to any such adverse findings to the satisfaction of the OfS: a. non-compliance with consumer protection law, as found by a court of England and Wales or competent authority; b. the offence provided for in section 214(1) of the Education Reform Act 1988 (unrecognised degrees); c. the offence provided for in section 76(6) of the Companies Act 2006 (failure to comply with a Secretary of State direction to change a company name); or d. the offence provided for in section 1198 of the Companies Act 2006 (name giving misleading indication of activities).
criteria.threshold: Must have addressed any adverse legal findings related to education provision to OfS satisfaction
risk_indicators: Court findings of consumer protection law violations in education context
Prosecutions under Education Reform Act 1988 for unrecognised degrees
Company name violations under Companies Act 2006 requirements
Outstanding legal findings that have not been remedied to OfS satisfaction

criteria.text: In the course of the provider's application for registration with the OfS (and the OfS's consideration of that application), the mere removal of a term, provision or any form of information from key documents or from any information about the provider will be insufficient to demonstrate that the provider, if registered, will treat its students fairly in accordance with C5.3, unless it can demonstrate that it has addressed any underlying issues related to that term, provision or form of information to the satisfaction of the OfS.
criteria.threshold: Must demonstrate substantive resolution of unfair treatment issues, not just removal of problematic terms
risk_indicators: Recent removal of terms from student contracts or policies without evidence of systemic change
Pattern of reactive changes to documentation only when challenged
Lack of evidence of cultural or procedural changes to prevent recurrence of unfair practices
Inadequate explanation of how underlying fairness issues have been addressed

conditions.notes: Scope includes provider relationships with students, higher education provision, ancillary services, and arrangements to attract or communicate with students including advertising and marketing. References to provision include offering provision. OfS will consider enforcement undertakings and outstanding enforcement applications when determining compliance.

conditions.title: Consumer protection and student contracts

criteria.text: The registered provider must comply with all applicable consumer protection law in its dealings with students in relation to higher education courses and, where applicable, ancillary services.
criteria.threshold: Full compliance with consumer protection law in all student dealings
risk_indicators: Student complaints alleging unfair contract terms or misleading information
Enforcement action or warnings from Trading Standards or CMA
Patterns of student refund disputes or contractual breaches
Failure to provide clear, accessible terms and conditions

criteria.text: The registered provider must have and comply with fair and transparent procedures for handling complaints from students.
criteria.threshold: Fair, transparent complaints procedures that are followed consistently
risk_indicators: High volume of unresolved student complaints or escalations to OIA
Complaints procedures that are unclear, inaccessible or not widely publicized
Evidence of bias or unfairness in complaint handling processes
Failure to respond to complaints within published timeframes

criteria.text: The registered provider must not engage in conduct that could be reasonably regarded as harassment of students or former students.
criteria.threshold: No conduct that could reasonably be regarded as harassment
risk_indicators: Reports of aggressive debt collection practices against students
Excessive or persistent contact with students who have raised complaints
Intimidatory behavior towards student representatives or whistleblowers
Use of legal threats to discourage legitimate complaints or criticism

criteria.text: The registered provider must not engage in the prohibited behaviors set out in the OfS prohibited behaviours list.
criteria.threshold: No engagement in any OfS prohibited behaviors
risk_indicators: Evidence of behaviors listed in the OfS prohibited behaviours document
Patterns of conduct that could constitute unfair commercial practices
Misleading marketing or recruitment practices
Failure to honor contractual commitments to students

conditions.notes: This condition includes detailed definitions for ancillary services, consumer protection law (covering Consumer Rights Act 2015, Consumer Contracts Regulations 2013, and other relevant legislation), competent authority, enforcement body, former student, prospective student, and key documents. The OfS prohibited behaviours list is a separate document that forms part of this condition. Applies to all providers seeking registration as an initial condition under section 5 of HERA.

conditions.title: Condition C5.1

criteria.text: Where this condition refers to a 'former student' who has an ongoing complaint against the provider, the OfS would consider whether the student raised their complaint within time limits specified by the provider and whether the time limit set was reasonable.
criteria.threshold: Ensure former students with ongoing complaints raised them within reasonable time limits
risk_indicators: Former students reporting unreasonably short complaint time limits
High number of complaints rejected solely on time limit grounds
Inconsistent application of time limits across different complaint types
Lack of clear communication about complaint deadlines to students

criteria.text: 'Ancillary services' includes (but is not limited to) library services, disability support packages, scholarships, accommodation and sports facilities, where there is a contract between a higher education provider and a student. This applies even where a contract or draft contract exists but is not yet in use. For example, this may be the case where services are newly introduced and not yet being delivered, or where students have not yet signed the relevant contract. This is because C5.2d. states that the provision of ancillary services includes the offering of those services and C5.2e states that reference to key documents (including contracts) includes any draft or proposed versions.
criteria.threshold: Ensure all ancillary services under contract with students comply with condition requirements
risk_indicators: Draft contracts for ancillary services lacking proper consumer protection terms
Misleading information about availability of ancillary services not yet operational
Student complaints about promised ancillary services not being delivered
Inadequate disclosure of terms and conditions for ancillary services

criteria.text: Where ancillary services are offered by a third party and the contract for services is between a student and that third party, this falls outside the scope of the condition. Where a provider works with recruitment agents or other entities similarly working on its behalf, it will be held accountable for their behaviour in relation to the 'fake reviews' provision in the OfS prohibited behaviours list. The OfS expects a provider to undertake appropriate due diligence on all third parties and on all third parties' arrangements, and remains responsible for any contracts it enters into with any third parties.
criteria.threshold: Conduct appropriate due diligence on third parties and remain accountable for their behavior regarding prohibited practices
risk_indicators: Evidence of fake reviews or misleading information from recruitment agents
Inadequate due diligence processes for third party contractors
Third parties acting on provider's behalf engaging in prohibited behaviors
Lack of oversight or monitoring of third party activities

criteria.text: In cases where two (or more) providers share ancillary services (for example, where one provider delivers services to another provider's students), these fall within the scope of the condition for whichever provider holds a contract with the student for these services. The same expectations in relation to appropriate due diligence apply in these shared arrangements.
criteria.threshold: Ensure shared ancillary service arrangements include proper due diligence and clear contractual responsibility
risk_indicators: Unclear contractual responsibility in shared service arrangements
Student confusion about which provider to contact for shared service issues
Inadequate quality assurance of shared ancillary services
Disputes between providers affecting service delivery to students

criteria.text: Higher education provided 'in any manner or form' includes any higher education course. This includes courses at any level and with any volume of learning, and it applies whether or not a course is recognised for OfS funding purposes, or any other purpose. This means, for example, that postgraduate research courses, the study of modules or courses leading to microcredentials, and apprenticeships, all fall within the scope of this condition. It also includes courses provided face-to-face, by distance learning, or a combination of delivery approaches.
criteria.threshold: Apply condition requirements to all higher education provision regardless of format, level, or recognition status
risk_indicators: Different compliance standards applied to different course types or delivery methods
Microcredential or apprenticeship programs lacking proper consumer protections
Distance learning courses with inadequate information disclosure
Inconsistent application of condition requirements across all provision types

criteria.text: Higher education provided 'by, or on behalf of, a provider' includes courses where students are, or will be, in any of the following categories: • taught by the provider seeking registration • registered with the provider seeking registration • studying for an award of the provider seeking registration (including where these services are provided on that provider's behalf).
criteria.threshold: Ensure condition compliance for all students taught by, registered with, or studying for awards of the provider
risk_indicators: Different information standards for students in different registration categories
Confusion among students about which provider is responsible for their consumer rights
Inadequate consumer protection for students studying for provider's awards through partnerships
Inconsistent complaint procedures across different student categories

criteria.text: The condition applies to all higher education provided through all forms of partnership arrangements. This includes instances where there is shared contractual responsibility for a student; this may be the case in a subcontractual partnership. In practice, this may result in more than one provider being responsible for compliance with this condition in relation to the same student. The OfS will assess a provider based on the arrangements it intends to have in place if it is registered. This includes whether it intends only to teach students registered by another provider (for example, through a subcontractual arrangement) or to teach students who it will also register (for example, through a validation arrangement).
criteria.threshold: Ensure condition compliance across all partnership arrangements and clearly define shared responsibilities
risk_indicators: Unclear responsibility allocation in partnership arrangements causing student detriment
Students receiving conflicting information from different partners
Gaps in consumer protection due to shared contractual arrangements
Inadequate coordination between partners on condition compliance

criteria.text: 'Information about the provider' includes anything individuals may rely on in their decision making about whether (or what) to study at the provider: for example, emails or other forms of communication; presentations delivered at open days; any written material used to inform communications (such as scripts for recruitment phone calls).
criteria.threshold: Ensure all information that prospective students may rely on for decision-making is accurate and compliant
risk_indicators: Misleading information in recruitment materials or communications
Inconsistent information provided across different communication channels
Scripts or materials used by recruitment staff containing inaccurate claims
Open day presentations making promises that cannot be delivered

criteria.text: Arrangements the provider 'plans to make' and 'proposed information' include the situation where a provider applying to register is not yet operating or not yet delivering higher education.
criteria.threshold: Ensure compliance with condition requirements even for planned or proposed arrangements and information
risk_indicators: Proposed arrangements lacking adequate consumer protection measures
Planned information disclosure processes that are inadequate or incomplete
Unrealistic promises about future provision in pre-operational phase
Insufficient planning for condition compliance before commencing operations

conditions.notes: This appears to be explanatory notes for Condition C5.1, providing definitions and clarifications for key terms and scope. The condition relates to consumer protection requirements for higher education providers, covering aspects such as complaint procedures, ancillary services, third party arrangements, partnership responsibilities, and information provision across all forms of higher education delivery.

conditions.title: Consumer protection compliance

criteria.text: The condition applies to relationships between a provider and its students, whether the latter pay for higher education or ancillary services directly or indirectly (for example, through Student Loans Company funding). This includes circumstances where a third party pays (for example, an employer or other sponsor).
criteria.threshold: Compliance with consumer protection requirements across all payment arrangements
risk_indicators: Inconsistent application of consumer protection policies across different payment types
Complaints from students or sponsors about unfair treatment based on funding source
Lack of clear policies covering third-party payment arrangements

criteria.text: This condition applies to a provider's relationships with students studying for the purpose of their business, trade or profession. This includes, for example, apprentices or other students who are studying as part of employer-sponsored programmes.
criteria.threshold: Consumer protection compliance for all professional and vocational students
risk_indicators: Different standards of consumer protection for apprentices versus other students
Employer-sponsored students receiving inadequate consumer protection information
Unclear responsibilities between employer, provider, and student in dispute resolution

criteria.text: Higher education and ancillary services are considered 'services' regardless of whether fees are charged and whether it is provided on a 'for profit' or a 'not for profit' basis.
criteria.threshold: Consumer protection applied universally regardless of fee structure or organizational status
risk_indicators: Different consumer protection standards for free versus fee-paying services
Inconsistent application of consumer law based on provider's profit status
Students unaware that free services are still subject to consumer protection requirements

criteria.text: The condition applies wherever higher education and ancillary services are offered. This therefore relates to the provider's arrangements to attract, encourage and communicate with students, and includes instances where a provider is not yet delivering such services.
criteria.threshold: Consumer protection compliance from marketing through to service delivery
risk_indicators: Misleading marketing materials or pre-enrollment communications
Lack of consumer protection oversight for recruitment and marketing activities
Inconsistent consumer protection standards across different delivery locations or methods

conditions.notes: This condition establishes the broad scope of consumer protection requirements, covering all student relationships regardless of payment method, study purpose, fee structure, or delivery stage. It emphasizes that consumer protection applies from initial marketing contact through to service delivery, across all locations and arrangements.

conditions.title: Condition C5.3

criteria.text: The overarching obligation of the condition is that a provider must treat its students fairly.
criteria.threshold: Must treat all students fairly in all activities
risk_indicators: Student complaints about discriminatory treatment or unfair processes
Inconsistent application of policies across different student groups
Lack of clear appeals procedures or student grievance mechanisms
Evidence of bias in academic assessment or student support services

criteria.text: The OfS will consider any of the provider's activities that are connected with providing higher education or ancillary services.
criteria.threshold: Fair treatment must extend to all higher education and ancillary services
risk_indicators: Unfair practices in accommodation services or student facilities
Discriminatory treatment in careers services or student support
Inconsistent charging or access policies for additional services
Complaints about unfair treatment in non-academic student services

conditions.notes: Unfair treatment is defined in the condition and is separate from the protections offered by consumer protection law. The OfS expects any higher education provider seeking registration to ensure it understands and complies with its legal obligations.

conditions.title: Fair treatment of students - prohibited behaviours and detriment tests

criteria.text: The OfS will deem that a provider does not treat students fairly where its actions (or its failure to act) fall within the descriptions in a specified list of behaviours (the OfS prohibited behaviours list)
criteria.threshold: Provider must avoid all behaviours listed in the OfS prohibited behaviours list
risk_indicators: Provider displays or presents inaccurate or false information to students
Provider's actions match descriptions in the OfS prohibited behaviours list
Evidence of unfair terms and conditions in contracts, even if not currently in use
Provider's communications or documentation contain misleading statements about courses or services

criteria.text: The OfS will deem that a provider does not treat students fairly where its actions (or its failure to act) give rise to actual or likely detriment to students
criteria.threshold: Provider must not cause actual or likely detriment to current, prospective or former students
risk_indicators: Student complaints indicating financial, academic or personal harm
Ambiguous, unclear or silent contract terms that could disadvantage students
Website evidence contradicting contractual obligations to students
Patterns of student withdrawals or transfers citing unfair treatment

conditions.notes: The tests at C5.4a and C5.4b are separate and distinct. Prohibited behaviour triggers non-compliance regardless of whether detriment occurs. 'Proposed' actions include unfair terms in unused contracts. 'Likely' relates to circumstances where evidence indicates potential unfair treatment despite no express proposal. The OfS prohibited behaviours list is published separately and forms part of initial registration requirements.

conditions.title: Student Protection

criteria.text: The registered provider must have adequate and effective arrangements to protect the interests of all students and ensure they are treated fairly.
criteria.threshold: Adequate and effective student protection arrangements demonstrated
risk_indicators: Absence of comprehensive student protection plans
Multiple student complaints about unfair treatment unresolved
Failure to communicate clearly about course changes or closures
Inadequate financial protection for student fees

criteria.text: The provider must not engage in conduct that could reasonably be regarded as representing poor value for students, bearing in mind the need to protect the academic experience and interests of students.
criteria.threshold: No conduct representing poor value for students
risk_indicators: Significant reduction in teaching hours without fee adjustment
Deterioration in facilities or resources without mitigation
Cancellation of advertised course features or modules
Staff-to-student ratios falling below sector norms

criteria.text: The provider must take all reasonable steps to mitigate for students any detriment, including curtailment of courses, or cessation of the provider's activities.
criteria.threshold: All reasonable steps taken to mitigate student detriment
risk_indicators: Lack of contingency plans for course continuation
Failure to arrange teach-out provisions
Inadequate notice periods for course changes
No alternative study arrangements offered to affected students

conditions.notes: This condition includes provisions informed by consumer protection law but may have different effects in the higher education context. 'Detriment' means any harm, damage or loss experienced by students (current, prospective and former). The OfS considers whether detriment and related actions would be reasonable in circumstances, taking into account necessity, provider control, and steps taken to limit detriment. Factors include emergency situations, provider control over circumstances, and measures to limit student detriment.

conditions.title: Student Protection - Fair Treatment Following Findings of Wrongdoing

criteria.text: The OfS's starting presumption is that a provider does not treat students fairly if it has been subject to findings of non-compliance with consumer protection law, or other wrongdoing as specified in C5.5. The OfS will consider findings made by UK courts or other competent authorities as defined in the condition.
criteria.threshold: No findings of non-compliance with consumer protection law or other specified wrongdoing by UK courts or competent authorities
risk_indicators: Court judgments against the provider for consumer protection law breaches
Findings by Trading Standards or other competent authorities regarding unfair trading practices
Regulatory action by sector-specific bodies for non-compliance with consumer protection requirements
Multiple student complaints to consumer protection agencies resulting in formal findings

criteria.text: Where there are findings of wrongdoing, the OfS will consider evidence submitted by the provider to reach a judgement. The OfS will consider the following non-exhaustive factors: the recency of the findings; whether the findings relate to matters that were repeated or sustained; whether the findings include a view about the deliberateness of the provider's actions or inaction; how the provider has engaged with the issue since the finding was made; the steps it has taken to address the issue and ensure it does not happen again in future.
criteria.threshold: Demonstrate satisfactory engagement with findings and implementation of adequate remedial measures
risk_indicators: Recent or repeated findings of wrongdoing without evidence of remediation
Failure to engage constructively with authorities following adverse findings
Absence of documented corrective action plans or preventive measures
Evidence of deliberate non-compliance or sustained patterns of wrongdoing

conditions.notes: The Protection from Harassment Act 1997 may be relevant to cases where a provider aggressively pursues academic sanctions for non-payment of non-tuition fee debts. Section 214(1) of the Education Reform Act 1988 relates to offering unrecognised degrees. Companies Act 2006 provisions relate to misleading company names including claiming to be a 'university' without permission. The OfS will consider findings relating to any form of education provision, including further education and ancillary services, not just higher education.

conditions.title: Undertakings or applications for enforcement orders

criteria.text: The existence of undertakings or applications for enforcement orders that relate to the provision of education and/or ancillary services will not automatically lead to a conclusion that the provider does not treat students fairly, as there is no presumption of wrongdoing.
criteria.threshold: Provider must provide information and evidence to demonstrate that issues leading to undertakings or enforcement order applications are not of concern
risk_indicators: Multiple or repeated undertakings with enforcement bodies
Failure to provide adequate information or evidence to address enforcement concerns
Ongoing issues identified in undertakings that remain unresolved
Pattern of consumer protection issues across education and ancillary services

conditions.notes: The OfS will consider information submitted by the provider alongside other reasonably available evidence to reach a judgement. The context extends beyond higher education to include further education provision. Enforcement bodies include the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) and Trading Standards. This condition relates specifically to applications for enforcement orders by enforcement bodies, not to enforcement orders issued by a court (which are covered under C5.5).

conditions.title: Student contracts and terms

criteria.text: During the application process, if there are terms or information of concern to the OfS in any of the provider's documents, simply removing these will not be sufficient, unless the provider can demonstrate that it has addressed any underlying issues associated with these terms.
criteria.threshold: Demonstrate that underlying issues have been addressed beyond mere removal of concerning terms
risk_indicators: Provider removes concerning terms without demonstrating understanding of why they were problematic
Similar unfair terms or statements appear elsewhere in provider documentation or website
Provider fails to replace removed terms with more suitable alternatives
No evidence of systemic changes to address root causes of unfair terms

criteria.text: The OfS will assess the documents the provider submits with its application and will also consider any information published by the provider on its website, including information about courses, fees, ancillary services, affiliation with other bodies, and materials to attract or communicate with students.
criteria.threshold: All submitted documents and published information must treat students fairly and be consistent
risk_indicators: Inconsistencies between application documents and website information
Misleading advertising or marketing materials
Unclear or inaccurate information about fees, courses, or services
Third-party complaints or notifications about unfair treatment

criteria.text: Providers in partnerships share a responsibility to treat students fairly, including ensuring through their own due diligence processes that the other partner also treats them fairly. Any provider applying for registration must ensure that any information it publishes is clear, accurate and consistent with that shared by its partner.
criteria.threshold: Demonstrate due diligence in partnership arrangements and ensure consistent, fair treatment across all partners
risk_indicators: Inconsistent information between delivery provider and lead provider documentation
Lack of clear due diligence processes for partner arrangements
Disputes or unclear allocation of responsibilities between partners
Evidence that partner organizations engage in unfair student treatment

conditions.notes: The OfS will consider the consequences of term removal, whether removal addresses underlying issues, and whether other related concerns remain. Assessment may include information from third parties, OIA, CMA, or Trading Standards. In all circumstances, the test is whether the provider will treat students fairly if registered. Particular attention is paid to subcontractual arrangements where delivery providers must submit lead provider documents and work to address any concerning provisions.

conditions.title: Parse error — review manually
conditions.notes: condition C1 is likely to result in a judgement that initial

conditions.title: Consumer Protection

criteria.text: The provider must comply with consumer protection law and treat students fairly
criteria.threshold: Full compliance with consumer protection law and fair treatment of students
risk_indicators: Referrals to National Trading Standards
Student complaints about misleading information or unfair practices
Evidence of non-compliance with consumer protection regulations
Pattern of unresolved disputes with students over financial or contractual matters

conditions.notes: Regulatory interventions such as referrals to National Trading Standards will be considered in the OfS's assessment of compliance. This condition relates to ensuring providers meet their obligations under consumer protection law and maintain fair dealings with students.

conditions.title: Fair treatment of students

criteria.text: Where the OfS considers this initial condition satisfied, but identifies an increased risk of not treating students fairly, it may impose one or more specific ongoing conditions of registration, and will also consider whether additional monitoring requirements are appropriate.
criteria.threshold: Ongoing compliance monitoring when increased risk identified
risk_indicators: Provider changes student registration arrangements after initial approval
Subcontractual partnership arrangements modify student treatment provisions
Consumer protection law compliance issues emerge post-registration
OIA or other regulatory bodies raise concerns about student treatment

criteria.text: For example, where a provider does not intend to register any students when it seeks registration (because students will register with a lead provider in a subcontractual partnership), the OfS may require the provider to submit a reportable event if this position changes once it is registered.
criteria.threshold: Notification required when student registration arrangements change
risk_indicators: Provider begins direct student registration without notification
Changes to lead provider arrangements not reported
Subcontractual partnership modifications affecting student registration
New contractual documents introduced without OfS submission

criteria.text: This may include requiring the provider to submit the contractual and other documents it intends to use in its relationships with students.
criteria.threshold: Submit all student-facing contractual documents as required
risk_indicators: Contractual documents not submitted when requested
Student-facing documents modified without OfS notification
Terms and conditions changes implemented without approval
Documentation gaps in student relationship materials

conditions.notes: Any assessment that the OfS makes about whether a provider has satisfied this condition is not a judgement about whether the provider is complying with consumer protection law, and should not be seen as such. Providers will still need to seek their own legal advice to ensure compliance with the law. The OfS's assessment is also separate from the assessment of any other body (for example the OIA) and any judgement such a body may independently take about a provider and its documents.
